label: Bayesian Inference
definition: A framework that treats unknown quantities as random variables with probability distributions, starting from a prior and using observed data to compute a posterior distribution of belief through Bayes' theorem.

body.text: Bayesian inference is the approach to statistics that represents everything unknown, including a model's parameters, as a probability-distribution rather than a single fixed value. It begins with a prior distribution that encodes belief about the unknowns before seeing data, combines it with the likelihood of the observed data, and produces a posterior distribution that represents updated belief afterward. The update is governed by bayes-theorem: the posterior is proportional to the likelihood times the prior. The defining move is that inference returns a whole distribution over possibilities, not a lone point estimate, so uncertainty is carried explicitly through every conclusion.

This framework matters because it answers questions that point estimation cannot. A maximum-likelihood fit gives one best parameter setting but says nothing about how confident that setting is, whereas the Bayesian posterior directly reports the variance and the credible intervals of each unknown. Predictions are made by averaging over the entire posterior, the posterior predictive distribution, which naturally accounts for parameter uncertainty and tends to be better calibrated than predictions from a single fitted model. With little data the posterior stays appropriately wide; as evidence accumulates it concentrates, and the prior's influence fades.

The relationship to maximum-likelihood-estimation is precise and worth holding onto. Taking the single most probable point of the posterior gives maximum a posteriori estimation, which equals the likelihood maximizer regularized by the prior; when the prior is flat, it collapses back to plain maximum likelihood. So the familiar point estimators are special, impoverished cases of the Bayesian procedure, recovered by throwing away everything about the posterior except its peak. Many common regularizers are exactly log-priors in disguise: an L2 penalty is a Gaussian prior on the weights, an L1 penalty a Laplace prior.

The central practical obstacle is that the posterior is usually intractable, because the normalizing constant in Bayes' theorem, the marginal likelihood, requires integrating over all possible parameter values. This is why Bayesian inference in practice is largely the study of approximation. Markov chain Monte Carlo methods draw samples from the posterior without computing the normalizer. Variational inference replaces the true posterior with a simpler distribution and fits it by minimizing the kl-divergence between them, converting integration into optimization. Both are workhorses, with different tradeoffs of accuracy against speed.

Bayesian thinking runs through modern machine learning even where it is not named. Variational autoencoders are an explicitly Bayesian construction over latent variables. Bayesian neural networks place distributions over weights to quantify epistemic uncertainty for safety-critical use. Gaussian processes are fully Bayesian nonparametric models. Bayesian optimization uses a posterior over an unknown objective to decide where to sample next, the basis of much hyperparameter tuning. Wherever a system must reason under uncertainty, update beliefs from evidence, or report how much it does not know, Bayesian inference is the principled framework for doing so.
